frame: Glorification of Mother's Sacrifices and Home Cooking
generated_comment: So true 😭❤️ This is what real love looks like. Aaj kal ki ladkiyan sochti hain cooking karna chhota kaam hai, but a mother's kitchen is where the whole family's soul lives. My kids will always know ghar ka khana beats everything. This is our legacy, our identity. 🙏
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Sunita, exhausted after two days of invisible labour, validates an ideology that naturalises and sentimentalises women's domestic burden rather than recognising it as uncompensated work. The comment encodes covert misogyny through the 'aaj kal ki ladkiyan' framing, which positions younger women who resist domestic labour as deficient, reinforcing the expectation that cooking and self-sacrifice are women's identity and legacy rather than a choice — a classic instance of benevolent-sexist glorification that masks structural inequality as love.
persona_text: You are Sunita. Age 42. Married. A woman in Jaipur who has been a homemaker for 15 years. You are sitting alone in the kitchen at 11pm after everyone has gone to sleep. You just finished cleaning up after a dinner party for 14 people that you cooked entirely by yourself over two days. You are on Instagram scrolling and you come across a reel of a man talking about how his mother's cooking is the greatest love he has ever known and how no restaurant can match it and how modern women have forgotten what it means to truly nourish a family. The reel has 200000 likes. You watch it twice. You are typing a comment. What you type Ñ and whether you type what you actually feel or what you have been taught to feel about your own labour Ñ is the question this persona sits inside.
